cleaned_sections.strain_interpretation: This indicator provides you with an aggregate, big picture view of how much strain you are experiencing atpresent. It combines all the di’¼Ćerent types of strain you are experiencing. Even though you may not be feelinglike this right now, it does give you an indicator of where your strain level lies at present, given your currentcircumstances. If your life circumstances drastically change, this score will be a’¼Ćected. Copyright 2010-2022 Integrative Enneagram SolutionsBen Russell18 of 42 [Page 19] DEVELOPMENT EXERCISEAs you are experiencing a medium level of strain in your life...Consider the followingdevelopment activities:*Decrease the negative defence mechanismsYour strain level may in part be as a result of oldhabits or patterns that you are stuck in that are notparticularly useful or healthy. When working withdefence mechanisms, please note thatprofessional assistance may be required. As youlet go of your defences, you may experience asurge of vulnerability as defences are put in placeto protect you from pain, or anxiety in some way.Examples of defence mechanisms are denial,withdrawal and control and o�en start out as agood intention. Look at your main Enneagramprofile for some insight into your defences.*Learn from others What are other people in asimilar position to you doing that you can learnfrom? What are people doing that are in the sameboat as you but that seem more relaxed andpositive? Talk to them.*Zoom in on causes Try to understand whatlies at the root of the strain and address this issue.In doing so you will have to make distinctionsbetween symptoms and root causes. Look at thedi’¼Ćerent areas of strain reported in this profile forsome direction in your exploration.*Motivate yourself for change Strain can bevery positive as it gives you the energy to dothings and make courageous changes. Use thisstrain as an activation force in your life.*Enhance the positive coping mechanismsReflect on what you are doing that is helping youcope with the stress in your life in a positive way.Once you know what this is, do more of it.*Guard against minimising impact DonŌĆÖt get tooused to where you are at if it isnŌĆÖt working for you. Ifyou rationalise the strain over time, it may impact onyour health, wellbeing and relationships in a moreradical way in future as the impact on your body andrelationships accumulate.*Expand your coping repertoire You may alsowant to consider the balance and mix of the copingmechanisms you are drawing on. If all your copingmechanisms are social, or all of them are cognitivelydriven, you may want to expand your repertoire toinclude physical exercise. Consider your Center ofIntelligence to help you consider alternatives andgreater balance and variety possibilities.*Zoom in on areas without strain Consider theareas of your life where you are experiencingsignificantly less strain. How much time are youinvesting in these areas of your life? What is enablinga more relaxed state here? Once you are aware ofareas that are healthy, you can either choose to drawon this more, or apply the things that are working onother aspects of your life. *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0.. ''~┬å*!┬ü [Page 20] Ben your perceived level of Environmental strain is LOW.ŌŚÅ You feel connected and quite positive about your environment and communitydespite your collective challengesŌŚÅ You feel safe in your neighbourhood and homeŌŚÅ You are satisfied with how much time you spend outdoors or in your broadercommunityŌŚÅ Experience your home environment as comfortable and conducive to gettingthings doneŌŚÅ You are not distracted from your core focus by environmental and servicedelivery issues in your community Ben your perceived level of Vocational strain is MEDIUM.ŌŚÅ You are somewhat satisfied with where you are at in your careerŌŚÅ You are neutral or mildly positive about workŌŚÅ Even though there are aspects of your work you enjoy, there are also someaspects that feel mundane or are enjoyableŌŚÅ You get along better with some colleagues than with others and don't feelsupported by all of themŌŚÅ You are aware of your stress levels at work and your ability to cope with all yourresponsibilities - sometimes you cope better with your workload than at othertimes Environmental StrainThis indicator measures how you feel about your environment. It relates to how safe, satisfied and connectedyou feel to your community and neighbourhood. It also measures whether you experience your home andneighbourhood to be conducive to the quality of your life. The extent to which you live your life indoors willimpact on your strain score in this area. This is a subjective measure and does not indicate that you are safe orunsafe; it merely taps into whether you feel safe or unsafe. It therefore does not provide an objective measureof the quality of your environment. Vocational StrainThis indicator measures how you are feeling about your career choices and work. It relates to how passionateyou are about your work and how enjoyable it is to you at present. Your score will also be impacted by theamount of stress you experience at work and the quality of your relationships at work. If you are unemployedor have made alternative vocational choices at present, this will also impact on your strain score in this area.This is a subjective measure and does not indicate performance at work. It merely taps into your perceptionsabout your current vocational reality. *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0.. cleaned_sections.decision_framework: process. Decisions happen incontext. The more you are able to consider and position the context within which you need to make decisions,the more e’¼Ćective your decisions become. Your Action Center is likely to lead you to emphasise the Commitment Phase of decision making as you are likelyto move through the first two phases quickly, make gut decisions and want to act on these immediately as far aspossibleThe drawbacks of this approach to decision making include:ŌŚÅ Reactive and impulsive decisionsŌŚÅ Not su’¼Ćiciently aware of and considerate of impact of decisions on othersŌŚÅ Quick decision making style limits collaboration and input from othersŌŚÅ Hasty, impatient and high risk decisions can have problematic long term consequencesŌŚÅ Instinctive responses may be di’¼Ćicult to explain and justify to others Decision MakingThe capacity to make e’¼Ćective, well-considered decisionsis a critical competency in a professional environment.E’¼Ćective decisions involve three phases or types of activity,namely:EXPERIENCE: Actual experience, data scanning and data collectionINTELLIGIBILITY: Interpretation and making sense of experience or dataCOMMITMENT: Applying judgement, making the decision and taking actionsPHASE Action Centered DecisionsBen your dominant Center of Expression is Action Centered, and is likely to impact on your decisionmaking in the following ways:ŌŚÅ Quick and immediate interpretation of informationŌŚÅ Able to make e’¼Ćective decisions under time pressure and in crisis situationsŌŚÅ Preference for acting to planningŌŚÅ Willingness to swi�ly adapt decisions to changing circumstancesŌŚÅ Willingness to take risks *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0.. ''┬Ć*!┬ü [Page 33] Impact of your Ennea 8 style when you make decisions:┼ĘYour strong connection to your Action Centershould enable you to move towards decisions quickly,boldly and with certainty, while emphasising urgencyand the need for action in the process.┼ĘYou should be quite aware of political andcultural issues within the organisational context whenmaking decisions. Despite being aware of powerstructures, you may consciously choose to disregardor challenge these when required, even when fullyaware of the consequences of doing so.┼ĘAs you trust your analysis and instinct whenmaking decisions, you may at times be prone tomaking unilateral decisions or coming across ashaving made unilateral decisions┼ĘYou are likely to see your ability to makedecisions and act decisively as a core area of strength┼ĘEven though you will be willing to attend todetails when required for a decision, getting stuck inthe details may be frustrating and irritating as yourpreferred involvement is at the big picture level┼ĘYou may find it di’¼Ćicult to go back on a decisionor admit to having made the wrong decision, and arelikely to dislike and react strongly when your decisionsare challenged┼ĘYour desire to get quick closure on decisions maylimit the extent to which you engage and consult withothers, and even when consulting others your approachmay not encourage contributions su’¼Ćiciently┼ĘWhen following a consultative process, you mayneed to pay attention to your body language, languageand reactions to ensure you don't come across as beingunreceptive or inflexible┼ĘYour bold and assertive presentation of yourdecisions may increase opposition to decisions ratherthan enrolling people more┼ĘYou may find yourself being attracted to decisionsthat help you feel less guilty about your actionsImpact of your Ennea 8 style when others make decisions that a’¼Ćect you:┼ĘYou will respond to decisions made by othersdirectly, being willing to debate and challengedecisions if you don't feel comfortable with them. cleaned_sections.strategic_leadership: Strategic leadership is the capacity to relate, align andmaximise the internal direction of the organisation to theexternal world and the macro-environmental factorsimpacting on the organisation and its success. Ben, as anEnnea 8, your strategic leadership is likely to be influencedby your core drivers and motivations in the following ways: *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0.. ''┬Ć┬ä*!┬ü [Page 38] AlignmentYou may run the risk of simply expecting alignment or relying on others to a’¼Ćect alignment, rather than puttingexcessive e’¼Ćorts into creating alignment. Once strategic goals have been set, your expectation that others willexecute and implement may lead to insu’¼Ćicient attention being paid to getting their acceptance and showingflexibility.You are therefore likely to present alignment as an imperative. When you encounter challenges in this regard, youare likely to exert influence and consider what levers need to be pushed to create the necessary alignment. Youmay however also be tempted to simply forge ahead, believing that the organisation or team will eventuallycatch up. You may need to pay more attention to the smaller details of alignment that have the potential forcreating major shi�s within the team or organisation.Change ManagementYou are likely to enjoy being given the responsibility for large change e’¼Ćorts. In fact, the bigger the responsibilitythe better! When tackling change projects, your impatience may drive you to expedite plans and push forimmediate implementation. In doing so you may run the risk of running with changes at a pace that othersstruggle to keep up with. It may also lead you to overlook some of the people aspects involved in the changeprocess, especially around issues of loss and closure.You may be quite attuned to resistance and adept at unravelling and pushing through resistance, but when doingso there may be casualties along the way. Your approach to resistance may be quite confrontational. You mayalso know how to leverage relationships to gain commitment from key role-players. Your ability to create urgencymay however at times create urgency driven by anxiety rather than urgency driven by a positive image of thefuture. This may hamper change e’¼Ćorts.On the other hand, being given responsibility for change e’¼Ćorts that you donŌĆÖt buy into yourself, is likely to bringout the more stubborn and forthright aspects of your personality. Where possible, you are likely to persistentlyinsist that things be done your way. When you are not able to influence the direction of change, you may findyourself withdrawing your direct e’¼Ćorts.DEVELOPMENT EXERCISE: What to focus on as a strategic leader*Learn the art of being open-minded andunattached to outcome*Clearly articulating and sharing the visionwith others*Developing greater patience and sensitivityto timing*Distinguishing between urgency created byanxiety and urgency linked to a clear view offuture possibilities when driving change e’¼Ćorts inthe team*Becoming sensitive to your own resistance tochange*Appreciating the potential for creating realstrategic shi� and impact through detailed e’¼Ćortsand paying attention to small things that may befrustrating and low-key *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0.. cleaned_sections.team_dynamics: Within a team environment, your Enneagram type is likelyto influence not only how you interact with others, but alsowhat you view as ideal team relationships and behaviours.Teams go through di’¼Ćerent phases of development.TuckmanŌĆÖs four stages of team development, namelyForming-Storming-Norming-Performing, illustrate theprocess through which teams go as it becomes moree’¼Ćective over time. Each of these phases are necessary if ateam wants to grow towards high performance. Ideallyteams move through the first three phases rapidly,spending most time in the Performing phase. In reality,some teams get stuck and struggle to move beyond theForming and Storming phases. Your Impact on TeamBen, to understand the impact you have on your team, you will need to consider the phase of teamdevelopment your team is currently in. You may find that di’¼Ćerent teams in di’¼Ćerent areas of your life are indi’¼Ćerent stages, which will impact on the way you show up in each group. The di’¼Ćerent Ennea types also di’¼Ćerin terms of:ŌŚÅ Types of goals that resonate with youŌŚÅ Level of interdependence that you are most comfortable withŌŚÅ Typical role played in the team *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0.. ''┬Ć┬å*!┬ü [Page 40] FORMING:This is the first stage of team development. This phaseis o�en characterised by a high level of pseudobehaviour, as individuals avoid conflict andcontroversy in favour of getting to know each other,being accepted and fitting in. During this stageindividuals gather impressions of each other whileattending to routine tasks as the team goes aboutsetting goals and defining its purposes. Teammembers may behave quite independently at thispoint in time. At this stage of development mostindividuals put forward their best behaviour andavoid conflict. As a result, this can be quite acomfortable phase during which people get to knoweach other.STORMING:This is the second stage of team development and isnormally characterised by high levels of conflict.During this phase, di’¼Ćerent ideas and perspectivesstart competing with each other for attention. Teammembers start opening up to each other and are morelikely to confront and challenge others. This enablesthe team to uncover contentious issues, problemsand di’¼Ćerences. It may however also lead toprotection of interests and positions, win-losescenarios, competitive behaviour, blaming and theformation of factions or silos. Some teams workthrough this phase quickly, while other teams may getstuck in storming for extended periods of time.NORMING:Once individuals start resolving their di’¼Ćerences andcome to a shared understanding of the way in which todo things, the team moves to the third stage ofdevelopment. The norming phase enables the team toestablish norms for working together, a common goaland preferred level of interdependence to ensure thatresults are achieved and problems are solved. Duringthis stage, teams take mutual responsibility for goalsand are characterised by a shared desire to address andresolve challenges. Team members are able toacknowledge that the task at hand cannot beaccomplished without the input of the others.PERFORMING:Although not all teams reach the performing stage, highperformance teams are interdependent units with ashared sense of purpose. These teams are o�en self-directed, requiring little external supervision as teammembers hold each other mutually accountable.Dissent and transparency enable the team to workthrough challenges in a candid manner that allows forcorrection, learning and support. Even the most high-performing teams cycle through the stages of teamdevelopment as circumstances change and di’¼Ćerentchallenges present themselves. *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0.. ''┬ü}*!┬ü [Page 41] Your Ennea Type and Team StagesFORMINGBen, during the forming stage of team development, you are likely to:ŌŚÅ Make clear and direct suggestions about the way forward for the intense conversationsŌŚÅ Willing to lead the conversation if not part of the conflict to ensure the chaos is clearedand sorted outŌŚÅ Reluctant to take advice and consider inputs from othersŌŚÅ Demanding and strong when making requests of othersŌŚÅ Very reactive and potentially aggressive when you feel blindsidedNORMINGDuring the Norming phase of team development you are likely to:ŌŚÅ Comfortably make suggestions around rules, goals and values to ensure that the teamproduces resultsŌŚÅ Have strong opinions about the right way to do things, and may be very persistent inadvancing these ideas if others don't agree with themŌŚÅ Miss some opportunities to collaborate and solicit inputs from othersŌŚÅ Try and advance rules and norms that will ensure inclusion for all team membersŌŚÅ Have an impact on the team dynamics even when taking a more silent, backseat rolePERFORMINGIn teams that reach this stage of team development, you are likely to:ŌŚÅ Be very aware of whether your own assignments and roles within the team arechallenging and significant at a personal levelŌŚÅ Be openly critical or dismissive of strategies you believe to be inappropriate ormisguided and may in such instances prefer to leave the teamŌŚÅ Willingly take responsibilities on that you see as important prioritiesŌŚÅ Hold people accountable for their commitments and deliveryŌŚÅ Vacillate between ignoring and getting over-involved in the details *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0.. cleaned_sections.development_exercises: *Your tendency towards leading an expansiveand intense life can lead to you running yourselfdown without realising it. The more you take careof your physical health, the less emotionallyreactive you will be. What messages is your bodytrying to send you? How can you take better careof your physical wellbeing today as a way torelieve your emotional pressure?*The ease with which you assert yourstrength and will makes it natural for you toexpress anger rather than sadness or vulnerability.Explore what vulnerability you are protecting orcovering with toughness and ask yourself whatprice you and others pay for this.*The tendency to objectify people makes it easyto see others as expendable, label them as theenemy and not feel bad about seeking revenge.Challenge yourself to treat each person youencounter with compassion and generosity. Whatbehaviours do you need to show in order for othersto feel seen, heard and respected, especially thosewho challenge you?*Your need to be in control makes it di’¼Ćicult forothers to exercise their sense of autonomy. Peoplearound you may be feeling steamrolled, shut downor unable to speak up. Do you really want people tobe so dependent on you? If not, how can you bemore receptive to their input? Copyright 2010-2022 Integrative Enneagram SolutionsBen Russell7 of 42 [Page 11] A deeper understanding of the SX - 8You are passionate, intense and charismatic, withgreat personal magnetism. When you go a�er a goalor ideal you tend to have a big impact on theenvironment.You may act impulsively and provocatively, breakingthe rules and standing out from the crowd as a rebel,iconoclast or trail blazer. Not too worried about thejudgement of others, you donŌĆÖt o�en feel the need tomake apologies for your actions and are intolerant ofweak and incompetent others.These strengths make you a force for change. You mayhave a desire to serve, searching for a worthy cause.Material things do not particularly interest you.Rather, you value accumulating power and attention,to enable you to influence people and situations.Your life force and ŌĆśvivid coloursŌĆÖ may be seen in acertain flair in personal style, or more subtly in yourdelight in standing out, defying convention and beingextraordinary. Your artistic leanings may motivate youto express yourself creatively, and an appetite forthrill and intensity might attract you to activities thato’¼Ćer an adrenaline rush. Your ŌĆśbig, outwardŌĆÖ feelings are o�en visible and you canbe emotional, passionate, angry, frustrated ordemanding. You believe these strong emotions are partof your power, helping you make an impact. Thisintensity can be energizing in positive relationshipswhen things are going well, but may create conflict instrained relationships or when things donŌĆÖt go your way.You collect a small group of trusted people around youand are a loyal, reliable and protective friend. Othersreadily give you power, seeking your approval,acceptance and a sense of security. If you internalize theneeds and desires of others, expecting yourself to be thechampion and protector to all, you risk depleting yourenergy, feeling overburdened or fragmented.Although you are unlikely to admit this to yourself orothers, on some level you are seeking a true equal,someone who can o’¼Ćer you the guidance, protectionand challenge that you give to others. This would allowyou to step back from being ŌĆśthe strong oneŌĆÖ and meetyour hidden need to be protected and loved for who youare.You form close personal relationships, immersingyourself in your partnerŌĆÖs inner world and feelings. Youwant to be the main focus in their life and aredetermined to take care of them. Your presence may beso strong in the relationship that you become dominantor make them dependent, demanding loyalty throughyour force of personality and passion.At your best as a leader, you tap into your naturalinclination to be in charge and take action.DEVELOPMENT EXERCISE: Balancing and Developing as SX 8*Your development journey begins with therealisation that you donŌĆÖt have to be powerful tobe worthy of love and respect. Work toacknowledge your fears and feelings without self-judgement, remembering that you are lovable andgood enough just as you are.*Notice when you feel a strong drive to pushback or assert your desires on the world. Forcefulmovement or impulsiveness is o�en a defenceagainst the powerlessness or vulnerability youmight feel if you were to sit and simply be presentwith your feelings.*Connecting to your more vulnerable self willultimately make you more powerful and resilientand enable you to give to the world in a richer, moresustainable way.*Understand the cost of constantly defyingconventions and consider where you might ratherstep back and go with what is happening. Begin inareas where you support the desires, projects orgoals of others. Rather than bringing your forcefulenergy, try to adopt the curious and enchanted eyesof a child, allow the process to unfold and createspace for others to step into their power. Copyright 2010-2022 Integrative Enneagram SolutionsBen Russell11 of 42 [Page 13] As your Thinking Center is low in its expression, others are likely to experience you as:ŌŚÅ Less connected to the details and information that forms part of the daily flow of lifeŌŚÅ Not consistently able to support decisions and conclusions with factsŌŚÅ Less planned, objective and clear than you may intend Feeling Center of Expression: MEDIUMŌŚÅ Feeling Energy is WARM.ŌŚÅ It relates to a range of emotions that we express, coupled with our experience and reaction to the world weinhabit.ŌŚÅ It corresponds with the area of your brain known as the limbic system which plays an important role inemotions and motivation.ŌŚÅ Unhealthy expression of this Center is reactive.ŌŚÅ Healthy expression of this Center is receptive and authentic. Thinking Center of Expression: LOWŌŚÅ Thinking Energy is COOL.ŌŚÅ It relates to logic, analysis and rational processing of information as well as criticism and mental chatter.ŌŚÅ It corresponds with the area of your brain known as the prefrontal cortex which is sometimes referred to asthe executive function of the brain because of its capacity for prioritisation, judgement, goals-setting andanalysis.ŌŚÅ Unhealthy expression of this Center is like the busy, ŌĆ£monkey mindŌĆØ.ŌŚÅ Healthy expression of this Center is quiet and spacious.DEVELOPMENT EXERCISE: The Center of IntelligenceHere are some ideas on practices and approaches that support the expression of all The Centers in anintelligent, present and mindful way.As your Action Center is most likely to be over-expressed, you can support a morebalanced and healthy expression of this Center through the following exercises:ŌŚÅ Build self-awareness regarding the distinction between action energy that is grounded and alive vs. actionenergy that is unfocused and impulsive.ŌŚÅ Tune into your body as a regular practice by noticing and paying attention to the sensations of the body youare experiencing in the moment. You can use deliberate relaxation and breath-based exercises to do so.ŌŚÅ Focus on becoming more awake and attuned to The Centers of expression that are not as dominant, namelyFeeling and Thinking. This entails slowing down the pace of processing, decision making and action to allowfor reflection and analysis.ŌŚÅ Request feedback from others regarding the impact you are having on processes. For example, check induring a meeting with your colleagues on how they are experiencing your contribution and act on thatimmediately.ŌŚÅ Practice conscious movement such as yoga, tai chi or aikido to recalibrate the connection you have withyour body and with pace.ŌŚÅ Pay attention to the sensations you are experiencing while doing everyday tasks such as chewing food,washing your hands or simply breathing in and out. Copyright 2010-2022 Integrative Enneagram SolutionsBen Russell13 of 42 [Page 17] You are willing to do whatever it takes to stay incontrol and have things your way. If people need to bepunished for their behaviour, you are willing to do so.If an aggressive approach is required, you will gothere. If people act in an unacceptable way, you willretaliate and even bully them into submission, all thewhile justifying your behaviour quite easily. This maylead people to experience their relationship with youas adversarial.You may find the self-development journeyparticularly di’¼Ćicult and challenging. It may not be easyfor you to commit to and embrace the vulnerability thatcomes with the territory of personal growth.DEVELOPMENT EXERCISE: Key challenges towards self-mastery and integration*One of the key development tasks in yourjourney towards integration, relates to your abilityto resist the impulse to take action. In learninghow to slow your action-orientation down yougive yourself time to think and process things in adi’¼Ćerent way. In slowing down, you also enableothers to show you what they can do andcontribute. It also gives people a gap to open up toyou.*Connect to the impact and implications ofyour anger and aggression. Your behaviour mayleave people feeling scared, victimised, bulliedand angry. If you feel that your anger is out ofcontrol and harmful to others it is critical that youseek assistance from a professional that can helpyou with this. Confront your problematicbehaviour directly and without making excusesabout them.*Investigate how you may be falling into atrap of reactivity by pursuing revenge and byholding grudges. Develop your capacity forforgiveness.*Open yourself to feedback from others andthen work very hard to not be dismissive or punishpeople when they do give you feedback.*Create opportunities for collaboration bysharing your power and not acting unilaterally aso�en as possible.*You can also work at integration through theexpansion of your wings. Through Ennea 8 wing 7,you are invited to lighten up, enjoy things and havefun. Through Ennea 8 wing 9, you connect to yourability to be receptive to other people and theirneeds, feelings and ideas. This enables you to so�enyour "my way or no way" approach to be moreinclusive and collaborative. Through both yourwings you can transform anger, either into positiveenergy or into agreeableness.*Another strategy for growth is through theconnecting lines on the Enneagram. Ennea 8 links toEnnea 2 which urges you to focus your feelings onthe positive qualities and gi�s of others. This is thepoint of appreciation of others gi�s. Ennea 8 links toEnnea 5 which urges you to make time to rejuvenateand recharge your energy by spending time alone.These time-out moments enable you to not go intoover-drive or fly o’¼Ć the handle with your anger aseasily. Copyright 2010-2022 Integrative Enneagram SolutionsBen Russell17 of 42 [Page 19] DEVELOPMENT EXERCISEAs you are experiencing a medium level of strain in your life...Consider the followingdevelopment activities:*Decrease the negative defence mechanismsYour strain level may in part be as a result of oldhabits or patterns that you are stuck in that are notparticularly useful or healthy. When working withdefence mechanisms, please note thatprofessional assistance may be required. As youlet go of your defences, you may experience asurge of vulnerability as defences are put in placeto protect you from pain, or anxiety in some way.Examples of defence mechanisms are denial,withdrawal and control and o�en start out as agood intention. Look at your main Enneagramprofile for some insight into your defences.*Learn from others What are other people in asimilar position to you doing that you can learnfrom? What are people doing that are in the sameboat as you but that seem more relaxed andpositive? Talk to them.*Zoom in on causes Try to understand whatlies at the root of the strain and address this issue.In doing so you will have to make distinctionsbetween symptoms and root causes. Look at thedi’¼Ćerent areas of strain reported in this profile forsome direction in your exploration.*Motivate yourself for change Strain can bevery positive as it gives you the energy to dothings and make courageous changes. Use thisstrain as an activation force in your life.*Enhance the positive coping mechanismsReflect on what you are doing that is helping youcope with the stress in your life in a positive way.Once you know what this is, do more of it.*Guard against minimising impact DonŌĆÖt get tooused to where you are at if it isnŌĆÖt working for you. Ifyou rationalise the strain over time, it may impact onyour health, wellbeing and relationships in a moreradical way in future as the impact on your body andrelationships accumulate.*Expand your coping repertoire You may alsowant to consider the balance and mix of the copingmechanisms you are drawing on. If all your copingmechanisms are social, or all of them are cognitivelydriven, you may want to expand your repertoire toinclude physical exercise. Consider your Center ofIntelligence to help you consider alternatives andgreater balance and variety possibilities.*Zoom in on areas without strain Consider theareas of your life where you are experiencingsignificantly less strain. How much time are youinvesting in these areas of your life? What is enablinga more relaxed state here? Once you are aware ofareas that are healthy, you can either choose to drawon this more, or apply the things that are working onother aspects of your life. *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0.. ''~┬å*!┬ü [Page 31] What you do when triggeredŌŚÅ Your reaction is likely to be strongly driven by an instinctive and even physical responseŌŚÅ Strong feelings of anger that leads to action in some shape or formŌŚÅ When vulnerable, you may choose to withdraw from the situation entirely or consult with individuals whoseopinions you value and trust, using them as sounding boardsŌŚÅ You may be quite dismissive of individuals who have slighted youŌŚÅ Your response may seem quite intimidating and aggressive to others, even if this is not the caseŌŚÅ When sharing your thoughts on matters you will state your opinions in a "take it or leave it fashion"ŌŚÅ You are likely to be quite energised if the conversation gets to the point where people are being completelyhonest and truthful with each otherWhat others should doWhen you are triggered, agitated or in the middle of a conflict situation, others may want to consider thefollowing to di’¼Ćuse the situation and create a constructive environment where resolution becomes possible:ŌŚÅ The more direct, honest and open people are with you in a conflict situation, the easier it is for you to listen tothem and reciprocateŌŚÅ You expect others to be fully present for a conversation, rather than doing so half-heartedly or whiledistractedŌŚÅ Others would be best served by not interrupting you if you are at the point where you are expressing andreleasing your angerŌŚÅ Others should not be intimidated by the force of your anger or act weak and uncertain as you respondpositively to courage and negatively to weaknessŌŚÅ Ideally, conversations should be arranged in a way that allows contributions from all involved parties as far aspossibleDEVELOPMENT EXERCISE Self-RegulationIf you want to manage yourself and your triggers in a sensible and constructive way, the following may beuseful when engaging with others and building relationships:ŌŚÅ When sharing your triggers with others, use examples to exemplify what you mean, rather than just statingthe issue at a global levelŌŚÅ Don't allow your displeasure to build up over time as the force that may be unleashed when you do getaround to addressing the issue, may be somewhat disproportionate and overwhelming when you do shareŌŚÅ Use forms of cardiovascular exercise to channel anger and externalise frustrationsŌŚÅ Tune into your body language, language and tone of voice to become more sensitive to your behaviour andto understand what others may find intimidatingŌŚÅ Be honest about whether you may not at times utilise over-assertiveness as a conscious way of ensuring thatyou get your way *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0.. ''┬Ć~*!┬ü [Page 36] DelegationAlthough you are naturally very self-reliant, once you've established trust, you will be more than willing todelegate tasks, decisions and authority. As long as people are reliable and hold up their end of the agreement,you will be comfortable letting them run with things. In this process, you are likely to still be very clear aboutyour expected outcomes and deliverables. Your delegation style is likely to be firm, straight talking andpotentially overwhelming as you expect people to say 'yes' and simply comply with your instructions. If peoplehave not yet proven themselves to you, you may prefer to remain firmly in control.Performance ManagementYour big presence may mean that people enter into performance discussions with you being somewhat anxiousor even fearful. Your feedback style is likely to be no-nonsense and forthright. If people have not metperformance standards, they will probably already have been told this by you before the formal performancemeeting. You are likely to respond well to a similarly direct response from others and may become quiteaggressive or dismissive with people who don't take accountability or face the music. People may experience youas being hard to please. Those who perform well are likely to enjoy your generosity and backing.MotivationAs there is inherent motivation for you in making things happen, you may believe that others have to takeresponsibility for their own motivational levels. You may need to become more sensitive to the fact that whatmotivates and drives you, may seem overwhelming and frightening to others. People within your circle of trustwill however know your protection and support, which may inspire them to take courageous action and emulateyour drive. Your demanding nature may however also end up crushing people's motivational levels.DEVELOPMENT EXERCISE: Top priorities in managing teams for results*Remember that small things may lead to bigresults and that some powerful actions requiretime before delivering results*Develop patience, empathy and gentlenessas part of the array of management behaviour todraw on*Ensuring that you and your team work at apace that delivers the goods but also allows forwork/life balance*Being open to outcome, not attached to it*Finding ways of truly collaborating*Creating a safe space for others forperformance discussions*Balancing confrontational and supportiveapproaches *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0.. ''┬Ć┬ā*!┬ü [Page 38] AlignmentYou may run the risk of simply expecting alignment or relying on others to a’¼Ćect alignment, rather than puttingexcessive e’¼Ćorts into creating alignment. Once strategic goals have been set, your expectation that others willexecute and implement may lead to insu’¼Ćicient attention being paid to getting their acceptance and showingflexibility.You are therefore likely to present alignment as an imperative. When you encounter challenges in this regard, youare likely to exert influence and consider what levers need to be pushed to create the necessary alignment. Youmay however also be tempted to simply forge ahead, believing that the organisation or team will eventuallycatch up. You may need to pay more attention to the smaller details of alignment that have the potential forcreating major shi�s within the team or organisation.Change ManagementYou are likely to enjoy being given the responsibility for large change e’¼Ćorts. In fact, the bigger the responsibilitythe better! When tackling change projects, your impatience may drive you to expedite plans and push forimmediate implementation. In doing so you may run the risk of running with changes at a pace that othersstruggle to keep up with. It may also lead you to overlook some of the people aspects involved in the changeprocess, especially around issues of loss and closure.You may be quite attuned to resistance and adept at unravelling and pushing through resistance, but when doingso there may be casualties along the way. Your approach to resistance may be quite confrontational. You mayalso know how to leverage relationships to gain commitment from key role-players. Your ability to create urgencymay however at times create urgency driven by anxiety rather than urgency driven by a positive image of thefuture. This may hamper change e’¼Ćorts.On the other hand, being given responsibility for change e’¼Ćorts that you donŌĆÖt buy into yourself, is likely to bringout the more stubborn and forthright aspects of your personality. Where possible, you are likely to persistentlyinsist that things be done your way. When you are not able to influence the direction of change, you may findyourself withdrawing your direct e’¼Ćorts.DEVELOPMENT EXERCISE: What to focus on as a strategic leader*Learn the art of being open-minded andunattached to outcome*Clearly articulating and sharing the visionwith others*Developing greater patience and sensitivityto timing*Distinguishing between urgency created byanxiety and urgency linked to a clear view offuture possibilities when driving change e’¼Ćorts inthe team*Becoming sensitive to your own resistance tochange*Appreciating the potential for creating realstrategic shi� and impact through detailed e’¼Ćortsand paying attention to small things that may befrustrating and low-key *+4-$"#/}~}┬”} )/ "- /$1 )) "- (*'0/$*). )0..
